main_summary.summary: Maria Varela, born January 1, 1940 in Newell, PA to a Mexican father from Mazapil, Zacatecas and an Irish-German mother, recounts her path from Catholic social-action organizing through SNCC field work and into the Chicano land grant movement. Interviewed June 29, 2016 in Pasadena by David Cline and Guha Shankar, she traces her formation in the Young Christian Students at Alverno College, her exposure to SNCC organizers Charles McDew and Tim Jenkins at National Student Association congresses, her participation in the 1962 Port Huron Conference with Tom Hayden and Al Haber, and Casey Hayden's recruitment of her to SNCC's Atlanta office. Sent to Selma in 1963 to support Edmundite priest Maurice Ouellet at St. Elizabeth Mission, Varela developed adult literacy programs and was arrested under Jim Clark's enforcement of city ordinances against the July 1964 Civil Rights Act. She designed photo-illustrated literacy booklets featuring the Panola County Okra Co-op, learned documentary photography from Matt Herron, and became one of nine SNCC photographers, covering the James Meredith March Against Fear. After 1966 she worked with Reies López Tijerina's Alianza in New Mexico, the Poor People's Campaign, and ultimately founded Tierra Wools, a Churro sheep livestock and weaving cooperative preserving Hispanic and Navajo land-based economies.
key_themes: SNCC adult literacy and movement publications
Documentary photography in the civil rights movement
Chicano land grant movement and rural cooperatives
main_summary.historical_significance:
Varela is one of nine SNCC staff photographers and the only Latina among them; her interview documents the bridge between the Black freedom movement and the emergent Chicano movement, including the Poor People's Campaign multicultural delegation. She provides primary-source testimony correcting the public narrative that SNCC expelled non-Black staff after 1966, citing a personalized letter from Stokely Carmichael and Ruby Doris Smith Robinson instructing her to continue her work with Fannie Lou Hamer.

Her account of producing photo-illustrated co-op booklets, film strips, and a documentary on César Chávez's United Farm Workers for distribution in Mississippi captures a less-documented dimension of SNCC's grassroots organizing infrastructure. Her later founding of clinics, co-ops, and Tierra Wools in northern New Mexico extends the empowerment model she learned in SNCC into Hispanic land grant communities, preserving the Churro sheep breed and Spanish-language weaving traditions.

chapters.title: Mexican Heritage and Pennsylvania Childhood
chapters.summary: Varela describes her birth in Newell, PA in 1940 to a Mexican father born in Mazapil, Zacatecas who came to the U.S. during the revolution, and her Irish-German mother. She recounts her grandfather's mining education at Columbia and Colorado School of Mines, anti-Mexican racism her father and uncles encountered, and the family's relocations.

chapters.title: Alverno College and Catholic Social Inquiry
chapters.summary: Varela describes attending Alverno College in Milwaukee, a Franciscan school for blue-collar Catholic women. She explains the European worker-priest movement, Pope Leo XIII's encyclical, and the observe-judge-act method of the Young Christian Students that shaped her organizing approach. She contrasts the Christian Family Movement and Sodality with the more activist YCS.

chapters.title: NSA, SDS, and Port Huron
chapters.summary: Varela describes hearing SNCC representatives Charles McDew and Tim Jenkins argue for sit-in support resolutions at National Student Association congresses. She recounts meeting Tom Hayden and Al Haber, joining the YCS national staff in Chicago, and being invited to the 1962 Port Huron Conference where she typed minutes and met Casey Hayden.

chapters.title: Joining SNCC in Selma
chapters.summary: Casey Hayden's letter brings Varela to SNCC's summer training school where Robert Coles introduced her to African American history. Frank Smith and Bernard Lafayette Jr. recruit her to Selma to support Edmundite priest Maurice Ouellet at St. Elizabeth Mission, conducting adult literacy work in preparation for voter registration's 21-question test.

chapters.title: Selma Literacy Work and Civil Rights Act
chapters.summary: Varela details her one-on-one literacy work with Mrs. Kathy in Selma, the 1964 summer recruitment of Black students for literacy program development, and the July 1964 arrests at the Thirsty Boy restaurant after Lyndon Johnson signed the Civil Rights Act. She describes Jim Clark's cattle prod, Selma City Council bans on SNCC and NAACP gatherings, and a warrant for her arrest.

chapters.title: Co-op Booklets and Mississippi Materials
chapters.summary: Varela describes producing photo-illustrated literacy booklets and film strips documenting the Panola County (Batesville MS) Okra Co-op so that Black farmers across the South could see Black leadership at work. She characterizes this as the 'second reconstruction' parallel to post-Civil War school-building and reframes the mainstream civil rights narrative to credit SNCC's post-1965 ground-level work.

chapters.title: SNCC Photography and the Meredith March
chapters.summary: Varela moves to Tougaloo, builds a darkroom with Bobby Fletcher, and trains for a week in New Orleans with Matt Herron on Ansel Adams gray-scale documentary technique. She becomes one of nine SNCC staff photographers, covers the James Meredith March Against Fear, and explains that SNCC photographers documented local Black leaders rather than movement celebrities. Their work is collected in This Light of Ours: Activist Photographers of the Civil Rights Movement.

chapters.title: Fannie Lou Hamer and Coalition Building
chapters.summary: Varela recalls helping Fannie Lou Hamer's MFDP campaign and recording her first autobiography with Julius Lester. She describes hog-cutting and chicharrones at Hamer's home, Hamer's distribution of Northern food donations to poor white Mississippi families, and Hamer's defense of accepting white volunteers in Freedom Summer. She also recounts coordinating SNCC's loan of Plymouth Valiants and CB radios to Marshall Ganz and George Ballis for the United Farm Workers, and producing a film strip on César Chávez's UFW for distribution to Mississippi Freedom Labor Union organizers.

chapters.title: Black Power and Latino Continuity in SNCC
chapters.summary: Varela disputes the public narrative that SNCC expelled non-Black staff after Stokely Carmichael's 1966 election, citing a personalized letter from Carmichael and Ruby Doris Smith Robinson asking her to continue work with Hamer. She names Elizabeth 'Betita' Martínez running the New York office and discusses why Bob Zellner's request to organize in the white community was declined. She explains that most SNCC staff were tactically rather than philosophically nonviolent, and reflects on how Black history work shaped her commitment to Black self-determination.

chapters.title: Reies López Tijerina and the Land Grant Movement
chapters.summary: Julian Bond's invitation to host Reies López Tijerina at the 1966 National Conference for New Politics leads Varela to move to New Mexico in 1967 to work for the Alianza. She describes the 1848 Treaty of Guadalupe Hidalgo protection of land grant heirs, Tijerina's charismatic but non-organized leadership style based in Albuquerque, and her unsuccessful proposal for a newsletter to connect Hispanic communities across northern New Mexico.

chapters.title: Poor People's Campaign and Multicultural Coalition
chapters.summary: Varela accompanies Tijerina and Corky Gonzales to the 1968 Poor People's Campaign in Washington. She describes the women's separate housing at a DC school, Chicano-led actions at the State Department, Department of Education hearings, and the Department of Justice on police brutality, plus relationship-building with Puerto Rican Young Lords, Brown Berets, and Appalachian delegates at the bridge crossing to Robert Kennedy's grave. She names Alicia Escalante of the LA welfare rights organization and questions SCLC's leadership of the campaign after King's assassination.

chapters.title: Northern New Mexico Clinics and Tierra Wools
chapters.summary: Anselmo Tijerina recruits Varela to support a community clinic in Tierra Amarilla after Anglo bankers attempt to burn it. The clinic, opening in 1969 with Presbyterian Church mortgage support, still operates today. Varela describes 30 years of work building alternative institutions in northern New Mexico — health care, cooperative agriculture, and livestock-and-weaving businesses that preserved the USDA-targeted Churro sheep breed central to Navajo and Hispanic weaving traditions.

chapters.title: DREAMers and Movement Legacy
chapters.summary: Varela reflects on the undocumented student movement's study of SNCC, the parallel between DACA setbacks and the 1964 MFDP defeat at Atlantic City, and Ella Baker's counsel to reflect rather than rush after major losses. She closes by saying the best of a movement may take 50 years to reach the next generation but persists if the empowerment foundation is solid.

loc_video.caption: Maria Varela oral history interview conducted by David P. Cline in Pasadena, California, 2016 June 29
